data.title: CHAPTER 13 — CAPSTONE: ONE WORKFLOW, THREE PROMPT PASSES
blocks: You have a recurring task. You have to summarize research literature for a working group that meets monthly. Every time,
Chapter 13 — Capstone: One Workflow, Thr — There is. And you have been building it since Chapter 1.

S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Apply the complete specification method to a real recurring task
· Run a structured output audit against defined criteria before revising
· Distinguish between failures of specification and failures of the model
· Produce a finalized prompt card that captures the workflow for reuse
· Recognize where human judgment remains irreplaceable in the loop
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

S03.meta: // SLIDE 03 — THE WORKFLOW: RESEARCH BRIEF F

data.title: THE WORKFLOW: RESEARCH BRIEF FOR A WORKING GROUP
blocks: Task:
Audience:
Recurring context:

data.title: PASS ONE: FIRST SPECIFICATION
blocks: · Key findings (3–5 bullet points, one finding per bullet, max 25 words each)
· Points of consensus (1 short paragraph, max 100 words)
· Open disputes or gaps in evidence (1 short paragraph, max 100 words)
· Implications for practice (3–4 bullet points, one implication per bullet)
· Every key finding must be traceable to at least one of the abstracts provided

data.title: PASS TWO: OUTPUT AUDIT
blocks: Each key finding traceable to an abstract — Check each bullet
Hedging level matches source hedging — Read carefully
Implications grounded in findings — Check each implication
Disagreements named, not synthesized — Read consensus section

data.title: PASS THREE: REVISED SPECIFICATION
blocks: Key findings (3–5 bullet points; each bullet . .)
· Points of consensus (1 paragraph, max 100 words; if sources disagree, name the dissent
· Open disputes or gaps (1 paragraph, max 100 words)
· Implications for practice (3–4 bullet points; each implication must name the finding
· Every key finding bullet must include a parenthetical source reference

data.title: THE FINISHED PROMPT CARD.
blocks: · 2–6 research abstracts (plain text, each clearly separated)
· No additional context required; audience and format are specified in the prompt
· Source references required on every key finding
· Hedging language must match source hedging verbatim
· Disagreements must be named explicitly, not synthesized

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE.
blocks: · Decide which abstracts to include (a judgment about relevance)
· Verify that source references in the output correspond to real abstracts (the model can hallucinate)
· Decide whether a named disagreement is worth flagging for the working group
· Determine whether the implications are actionable given the working group's specific situation

S09.meta: // SLIDE 09 — COMMON MISTAKES IN THE CAPSTON

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES IN THE CAPSTONE.
blocks: Revising before auditing.
Writing constraints so general they cannot be verified.
Using the capstone workflow on a one-time task.
Treating Pass Three as final.

data.title: FIGURE: THE AI WAYBACK MACHINE — THE PROMPT CARD'S ANCESTORS
blocks: SOP binder
style guide

peer review protocol

blocks: The first sign of trouble is usually not failure. It is fluency.
